description: Anacardium excelsum, the wild cashew, espavél or espavé, is a tree in the flowering plant family Anacardiaceae. The tree is common in the tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests of Pacific and Atlantic watersheds of Central and South America, extending as far north as Guatemala and south into Ecuador.
origin: COS OO, PAN, HON, ECU, COS

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: PAN, VEN OO, CARIBBEAN, WESTERN SOUTH AMERICA, VEN

description: Anacardium humile, a plant species from the genus Anacardium and the family Anacardiaceae, is known for its medicinal and insecticidal properties. The shrub is commonly found in the Brazilian Pantanal and Cerrado. Common names include monkey nut, dwarf cashew nut, cajui, cajuzinho-do-cerrado, and caju-do-campo.
origin: PAR, SOUTHERN SOUTH AMERICA, BZS SC, BZE, BZE PB

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical
care.tips: Native to: WESTERN SOUTH AMERICA, BZC DF, BZL ES, BZN RO, BZC GO

description: Cashew is the common name of a tropical evergreen tree Anacardium occidentale, in the family Anacardiaceae. It is the source of the cashew nut and the cashew apple. The tree can grow as tall as 14 meters.
origin: MXS OA, WIN GR, BZE PB, BZE RN, LEE SK
synonyms: ["Cassuvium solitarium", "Anacardium othonianum", "Anacardium rondonianum", "Anacardium subcordatum", "Anacardium kuhlmannianum", "Cassuvium pomiferum", "Anacardium occidentale var. americanum", "Cassuvium reniforme", "Anacardium occidentale var. gardneri", "Anacardium curatellifolium"]

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical
care.tips: Native to: NCB, BZN, MYA, SWC HC, PER

description: Astronium fraxinifolium is a hardwood timber tree, which is native to Amazon rainforest, Atlantic Forest, Caatinga, and Cerrado vegetation in Brazil. Common names include kingwood, locustwood, tigerwood, and zebrawood. This plant is cited in Flora Brasiliensis by Carl Friedrich Philipp von Martius. It is grouped with two other species of Astronium under the Portuguese name Gonçalo alves, also known as tigerwood.
origin: BZC MS, BZE AL, BOL, BZN AM, BZC GO

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical
care.tips: Native to: AGE SF, BZE BA, CLM, NORTHERN SOUTH AMERICA, BZL RJ

description: Astronium graveolens is a species of hardwood tree in the cashew family, Anacardiaceae, that is native to Mexico, Central America, and South America as far south as Bolivia. Common names include glassywood, tigerwood, ronrón (Spanish), aroeira (Portuguese), and Gonçalo alves (Portuguese), where it is grouped with two other species of Astronium.
origin: MEXICO, BZC MS, BZE AL, NIC, BOL
synonyms: ["Astronium graveolens var. inodorum", "Anisophyllum pinnatum", "Astronium graveolens var. dugandii", "Astronium graveolens var. planchonianum", "Astronium gracile f. acuminatum", "Astronium gracile var. acuminatum", "Astronium conzattii var. standleyi", "Astronium zongolicum", "Astronium gracile f. serratum", "Astronium conzattii var. lundellii"]

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical
care.tips: Native to: ECU, NIC OO, BZE BA, CLM, NORTHERN SOUTH AMERICA

description: Buchanania arborescens, commonly known as the little gooseberry tree or sparrow's mango, is a small and slender tree native to seasonal tropical forests of northern Australia, Southeast Asia, and the Solomon Islands.
origin: MLY PM, QLD QU, BIS OO, SUM OO, MLY
synonyms: ["Buchanania versteeghii", "Buchanania longifolia", "Buchanania longifolia", "Buchanania palembanica", "Buchanania papuana", "Buchanania lucida", "Buchanania petiolaris", "Buchanania florida", "Coniogeton arborescens", "Buchanania bancana"]

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: BOR, VAN, VIE OO, CBD, EASTERN ASIA

description: Buchanania obovata is a small to medium-sized understorey tree in woodlands native to northern Australia, in particular in Arnhem Land in the Northern Territory. Common names include green plum and wild mango.

Cotinus coggygria, syn. Rhus cotinus, the European smoketree, Eurasian smoketree, smoke tree, smoke bush, Venetian sumach, or dyer's sumach, is a Eurasian species of flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae.
origin: S. Central Europe to China
synonyms: ["Cotinus coggygria var. cinereus", "Cotinus coggygria var. parvifolia", "Rhus obovatifolia", "Cotinus coggygria var. pendulus", "Cotinus coggygria f. atropurpureus", "Rhus simplicifolia", "Rhus velutina", "Cotinus cinereus", "Cotinus coggygria var. velutinus", "Cotinus coggygria var. pubescens"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
care.tips: Similar plants: Cercis canadensis | Native to: NCS ST, NCS KC, CHN SX, EASTERN ASIA, NWY OO
common_problems: Although typically considered pest and disease-free, it has some susceptibility to rust, leaf spot, and verticillium wilt.
This plant is in the same family as poison ivy and contact with the resinous sap may cause contact dermatitis, especially to those sensitive to poison ivy.
common_pests: Although typically considered pest and disease-free, it has some susceptibility to rust, leaf spot, and verticillium wilt.

description: Dracontomelon dao, the Argus pheasant-tree, Pacific walnut, Papuan walnut, New Guinea walnut, paldao or simply dao, is a tree in the family Anacardiaceae, native to tropical Asia.
origin: MLY PM, EHM AP, SUM OO, BIS OO, CHC CQ
synonyms: ["Poupartia mangifera", "Dracontomelon lamujo", "Paliurus dao", "Dracontomelon puberulum", "Poupartia pinnata", "Dracontomelon mangiferum var. puberulum", "Dracontomelon mangiferum var. pubescens", "Comeurya cumingiana", "Dracontomelon edule", "Dracontomelon cumingianum"]

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical

description: Euroschinus falcatus is a large tree in the mango and cashew family Anacardiaceae, found along almost the entire east coast of Australia from Cape York Peninsula to Jervis Bay. Common names include blush cudgerie and maiden's blush.
origin: QLD, QLD QU, QLD CS, AUSTRALASIA, AUSTRALIA
synonyms: ["Rhus euroschina", "Sorindeia falcata", "Euroschinus falcatus var. typica", "Euroschinus parvifolius", "Euroschinus falcatus var. angustifolia"]

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical

description: Lannea acida is a shrub or small deciduous tree within the family Anacardiaceae. It is endemic to the Guinea and Sudan savannas of West and Central Africa.
origin: GUI, SEN, CMN OO, MLI OO, GAM OO
synonyms: ["Sorindeia lagdoensis", "Lannea lagdoensis", "Tapirira acida", "Odina acida", "Lannea buettneri", "Lannea glaucescens", "Calesiam acidum"]

description: Lithraea molleoides is a tree that is native to South America, specially in Argentina, Uruguay, Bolivia, and the Cerrado vegetation of Brazil.
origin: AGW SL, BZL TR, AGE CH, MXC PU, BZE CE
synonyms: ["Schinus terebinthifolia var. ternifolia", "Lithraea molleoides var. lorentziana", "Schinus ternifolia", "Lithraea gilliesii", "Lithraea lorentzii", "Lithraea aroeirinha", "Schinus molleoides", "Rhus commersonii", "Schinus leucocarpa", "Rhus meridionalis"]

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical

description: Malosma is a monotypic genus comprising the species Malosma laurina. The species is an evergreen tree or shrub belonging to the sumac family, Anacardiaceae, native to the Pacific coast regions of southern California and Baja California, as well as the southeastern California Channel Islands, and some of the Pacific islands of Baja California. It is known as laurel sumac and lentisco (Spanish).
origin: MXN, MXI RG, MXN SO, MXE AG, MXI GU

care.light_guide: Climate: subtropical

description: Mangifera indica, commonly known as the mango after its fruit, is an evergreen species of flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae. It is a large fruit tree, capable of growing to a height and width of 30 m (100 ft). There are two distinct genetic populations in modern mangoes – the "Indian type" and the "Southeast Asian type".
origin: BAH, YEM, CBD OO, INDIAN SUBCONTINENT, MOZ
synonyms: ["Mangifera siamensis", "Mangifera laurina var. deropong", "Mangifera cambodiana", "Mangifera indica var. compressa", "Mangifera indica var. bapang", "Mangifera maritima", "Mangifera indica var. domestica", "Mangifera mekongensis", "Mangifera indica var. dodol", "Mangifera indica var. kidang"]

care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical

origin: BOL, VEN, PER, COS, PER OO
synonyms: ["Mauria heterophylla var. humboldtii", "Sorindeia ovalifolia", "Mauria heterophylla var. puberula", "Mauria biringo var. granatensis", "Mauria glauca", "Sorindeia puberula var. venulosa", "Mauria heterophylla var. contracta", "Mauria biringo var. weberbaueri", "Sorindeia biringo", "Mauria biringo"]

care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical

description: Metopium brownei is a species of plant in the family Anacardiaceae.

Myracrodruon urundeuva is a timber tree, which is often used for beekeeping. This plant is native to Argentina, Brazil, Bolivia and Paraguay, and it is typical of Caatinga, Cerrado, and Pantanal vegetation in Brazil.

Pistacia atlantica is a species of pistachio tree known by the English common name Mt. Atlas mastic tree, Atlas pistachio, Atlantic pistacio, Atlantic terebinth, wild pistachio, and Cyprus turpentine tree.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

ja: ピスタシア・アトランティカ

description: Pistacia chinensis, the Chinese pistache, is a small to medium-sized tree in the genus Pistacia in the cashew family Anacardiaceae, native to central and western China. This species is planted as a street tree in temperate areas worldwide due to its attractive fruit and autumn foliage.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Pistacia lentiscus is a dioecious evergreen shrub or small tree of the genus Pistacia native to the Mediterranean Basin. It grows up to 4 m (13 ft) tall and is cultivated for its aromatic resin, mainly on the Greek island of Chios, around the Turkish town of Çeşme and northern parts of Iraq.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Pistacia mexicana, also known as Mexican pistache, American pistachio or wild pistachio is a species of plant in the family Anacardiaceae found in Guatemala, Mexico, and the United States (Texas). It is threatened by habitat loss.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Pistacia terebinthus, also called the terebinth, is a deciduous shrub species of the genus Pistacia, native to the Mediterranean region from the western regions of Morocco and Portugal to Greece and western and southeastern Turkey. At one time terebinths growing on the eastern shores of the Mediterranean Sea were regarded as a separate species, Pistacia palaestina, but these are now considered to be a synonym of P. terebinthus.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: The pistachio is a small to medium-sized tree of the cashew family. The tree produces seeds that are widely consumed as food. Pistachios are eaten whole, and can be used as an ingredient in desserts, chocolate, candy, ice cream, and as a paste or butter. As a tree nut, pistachios are considered a priority allergen that may induce allergic reactions in susceptible people.
origin: Transcaucasus, Afghanistan to China, Taiwan, and the Philippines

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Following the flowers, the female flower produces hairy drupes. They are ovoid, 0.25 inches in diameter, and turn red as they ripen. Each drupe contains a stone. The drupes are present from June to August.
origin: North America--Eastern Canada to Mexico

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used the root to produce a yellow dye. They used berries to make beverages. They also dried the leaves and mixed them with other plant leaves to form a smoking mixture. The leaves and bark were also used to tan leather.

description: Rhus chinensis, the Chinese sumac or nutgall tree, is a deciduous shrub or small tree in the genus Rhus. Growing to 6 m (20 ft) tall, it has downy shoots and leaves comprising several leaflets. These turn red in autumn before falling.
origin: Himalaya to Japan

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Rhus coriaria, commonly called Sicilian sumac, tanner's sumach, or elm-leaved sumach, is a deciduous shrub to small tree in the cashew family Anacardiaceae. It is native to southern Europe and western Asia. The dried fruits are used as a spice, particularly in combination with other spices in the mixture called za'atar.
origin: North America--Eastern Canada to Mexico

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used the root to produce a yellow dye. They used berries to make beverages. They also dried the leaves and mixed them with other plant leaves to form a smoking mixture. The leaves and bark were also used to tan leather.

description: 1/4 inch red fruit covered in red sticky hairs in clusters from Aug. to October on female plants
origin: U.S.A. to Canada

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) The leaves of this plant are a source of black ink. Pigment can also be obtained from the wood of this plant and if used in the textile, toy, and paper industry.

description: Rhus trilobata is a North American shrub in the sumac genus (Rhus) with the common names skunkbush sumac, sourberry, skunkbush, and three-leaf sumac.
origin: North America

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans valued the plant for its astringent qualities.  The fruits were eaten for stomach ailments and tooth aches and were dried and ground into a paste used to treat smallpox.

description: Rhus typhina, the staghorn sumac, is a species of flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae, native to eastern North America. It is primarily found in southeastern Canada, the northeastern and midwestern United States, and the Appalachian Mountains, but it is widely cultivated as an ornamental throughout the temperate world. It is an invasive species in some parts of the world.
origin: Eastern North America

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.edible_parts: The fruits are soaked in water to make a tart lemony drink from the absorbic acid found in the hairs covering the fruits.

description: Following the flowers, the female flower produces hairy drupes. They are ovoid, 0.25 inches in diameter, and turn red as they ripen. Each drupe contains a stone. The drupes are present from June to August.
origin: North America--Eastern Canada to Mexico

care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used the root to produce a yellow dye. They used berries to make beverages. They also dried the leaves and mixed them with other plant leaves to form a smoking mixture. The leaves and bark were also used to tan leather.

description: Schinus molle is an evergreen tree that grows to 15 m. It is native to an area from the Peruvian Andes to southern Brazil. The bright pink fruits of S. molle are often sold as "pink peppercorns", although it is unrelated to black pepper. The word molle in Schinus molle comes from mulli, the Quechua word for the tree. The tree is host to the pepper-tree moth, Bombycomorpha bifascia.

Schinus terebinthifolia is a species of flowering plant in the cashew family, Anacardiaceae, that is native to subtropical and tropical South America. Common names include Brazilian peppertree, aroeira, rose pepper, broadleaved pepper tree, wilelaiki, Christmasberry tree and Florida holly. The species name has been very commonly misspelled as 'terebinthifolius'.

Spondias mombin, also known as yellow mombin, hog plum, amra or cajazeira, is a species of tree and flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae. It is native to the tropical Americas, including the West Indies. The tree was introduced by the Portuguese in South Asia in the beginning of the 17th century. It has been naturalized in parts of Africa, India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, The Bahamas, Indonesia, and other Caribbean islands.

Spondias purpurea is a species of flowering plant in the cashew family, Anacardiaceae, that is native to tropical regions of the Americas, from Mexico to northern Colombia and the southwest Caribbean Islands. It has also been introduced to and naturalized to other parts of the American tropics, Southeast Asia, and West Africa. It is commonly known as jocote, which derives from the Nahuatl word xocotl, meaning any kind of sour or acidic fruit.
Tapirira guianensis is a species of plant in the family Anacardiaceae. It is native to Cerrado and Atlantic Forest (ecoregions) vegetation in Brazil.
Toxicodendron diversilobum, commonly named Pacific poison oak or western poison oak, is a woody vine or shrub in the sumac family, Anacardiaceae.
origin: central and eastern United States

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.toxicity_note: Non-toxic to Dogs, Non-Toxic to Cats, Non-Toxic to Horses. Signs: Pets can get oils on their coat and expose humans.
care.toxicity_symptoms: Pets can get oils on their coat and expose humans.

description: Tight clusters of greenish-white unripe berries appear in late spring. When ripened, they turn a tannish-white. They measure 0.25 inches in diameter and are pubescent. Birds are attracted to their fruit.
origin: central and eastern United States

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

description: Toxicodendron radicans, commonly known as eastern poison ivy or poison ivy, is a species of allergenic flowering plant. It has numerous subtaxons and forms both vines and shrubs. Despite its common name, it is not a true ivy, but rather a member of the cashew and sumac family, Anacardiaceae. It is different from western poison ivy, Toxicodendron rydbergii, and resembles a number of species.
origin: Southern Canada, North America to Central America, Central China

care.light_guide: NC State: Dappled Sunlight (Shade through upper canopy all day)
care.toxicity_note: Human: allergenic. Parts: Leaf, herb. (Source: TPPT/Agroscope)

origin: central and eastern United States

description: The female flowers form clusters of dangling, smooth, round, berry-like drupes. They measure less than 1/4 inch in diameter and contain one seed. The drupes ripen from green to a dull yellowish-white in the late summer. The berries may persist through the winter and provide food for birds and rabbits. The fruit is toxic to humans.
origin: Eastern Canada and East Central and Eastern United States

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)

ja: トクシコデンドゥロン・ヴェルニクス
